---
title: "Survival Analysis - Cox Model"
author: "R. Jerome Dixon"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
format: 
  html: 
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    embed-resources: true
    default-image-extension: svg
    dpi: 600
---

```{r load-libraries}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: hide

library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(magrittr)
library(spatstat)
library(tibble)
library(ggplot2)
library(purrr)
library(tidyverse)
library(huxtable)
library(reticulate)

(options)(scipen=999)

```

### Survival Model Dataset

```{r load-dataset}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true

set.seed(1997)

survival_model <- read_rds(here("data","survival_analysis.rds"))

survival_model %<>%
  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor) 
 
survival_model %>% 
  str()


```

#### Format Dataset

```{r format-dataset}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true

library(dplyr)
library(forcats)

survival_model %<>%
  mutate(outcome_final = ifelse(is.na(outcome_final), 0, outcome_final)) %>%
  mutate(INIT_STAT = as.factor(INIT_STAT)) %>%
  mutate(INOTROPES_TCR = as.factor(INOTROPES_TCR)) %>%
  mutate(PGE_TCR = as.factor(PGE_TCR)) %>%
  mutate_if(is.factor, ~fct_na_value_to_level(., "Unknown")) %>% 
  select(-c(TRANSFUSIONS_CAND))


survival_model %>% 
  str()

```

#### Target Label for Censoring (Right-Censored)

CatBoost uses an interesting dual encoding transformation for survival analysis. The sign (positive or negative) of the label column is used to distinguish between events and censored data.

Positive values indicate the time until the event, and negative values indicate the time until censoring.

```{r}

survival_model %<>%
  mutate(label = ifelse(outcome_final, days_total, -days_total))

```

#### Dataset Feature Columns

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true

model_cb <- survival_model %>% 
  select(!c(WL_ID_CODE, PT_CODE, days_total, outcome_final))

features_cb <- model_cb %>% 
  select(-label)

target_cb <- model_cb$label

evaluation_data <- survival_model %>% 
  select(c(WL_ID_CODE, PT_CODE, days_total, outcome_final))

cat_features <- names(features_cb)[sapply(features_cb, is.factor)]


```

#### Train and Test Splits

```{python catboost-r-data}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: hide

from catboost import Pool
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# Initialize data
X = r.model_cb
X_eval = r.evaluation_data

y = r.target_cb

cat_features = r.cat_features

# Split data into training and testing sets
X_train, X_test = train_test_split(X, test_size=0.2, random_state=1997)


vals = X_train.mode().iloc[0]
X_train = X_train.fillna(vals)
X_test = X_test.fillna(vals)

# Extract labels for training and testing sets
Y_train = X_train['label']
y_test = X_test['label']

# Remove label columns from X_train and X_test
X_train = X_train.drop(columns=['label'])
X_test = X_test.drop(columns=['label'])

# Split evaluation data into training and testing sets
eval_train, eval_test = train_test_split(X_eval, test_size=0.2, random_state=1997)

# Using this object for feature importance calculations
train_pool = Pool(X_train, label=Y_train, cat_features=cat_features)

# Using this object for feature importance calculations
test_pool = Pool(X_test, label=y_test, cat_features=cat_features)


```

#### Hyperparameter Optimization with Optuna

 We implement a stratified cross-validation process for our survival analysis model, where the data is stratified based on a combination of event occurrence and time. Let's walk through what we are doing::

Event Occurrence:

`event_occurred = Y > 0` creates a boolean array where each element is True if the corresponding element in Y is positive (indicating an event occurred) and False otherwise (indicating censoring).
Time Binning:

`time_bins = pd.qcut(np.abs(Y), q=4, labels=False, duplicates='drop')` bins the absolute values of Y into quartiles. This means your survival times are divided into four groups with roughly equal numbers of observations. The duplicates='drop' option ensures that if there are duplicate bin edges (which can happen if there are many identical values in Y), they will be dropped to avoid errors.


Stratification Labels:

`stratification_labels = np.array([f"{e}_{t}" for e, t in zip(event_occurred, time_bins)])` combines the event occurrence and time binning information into a single label for each observation. This label is a string combining the event indicator and time bin. For example, an observation with an event and in the first time bin might have a label "True_0".


Stratified KFold Cross-Validation:

`StratifiedKFold(n_splits=2, shuffle=True, random_state=1997)` initializes a stratified KFold cross-validator with 2 folds. Stratification is based on the labels created in the previous step, ensuring that each fold is representative of the overall distribution of both the event occurrences and the time bins.

The loop `for train_index, test_index in skf.split(X, stratification_labels)` then iterates over these folds. For each fold, it splits X and Y into training (X_train, Y_train) and testing (X_test, Y_test) sets based on the indices provided by the stratified splitter.


CatBoost Pools:

Inside the loop, `train_pool = Pool(X_train, label=Y_train, cat_features=cat_features)` and `test_pool = Pool(X_test, label=Y_test, cat_features=cat_features)` create CatBoost Pool objects for the training and testing sets, respectively. These Pool objects are used to train and evaluate the CatBoost model. The Pool object efficiently handles datasets with categorical features (cat_features).

By combining stratified cross-validation with CatBoost training, we aim to develop a robust survival analysis model that generalizes well to new data. We make take a small hit in model accuracy but this stratification strategy ensures that each fold of the cross-validation process is balanced in terms of both the event occurrence and the distribution of survival times, which is very important in survival analysis to prevent biased estimates.


```{python optuna-hyperparameter-optimization}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: hide

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedKFold
import pandas as pd
import catboost as cb
import optuna
from lifelines.utils import concordance_index
import numpy as np


# Convert target label from list to array
Y = np.array(y)


def objective(trial):
  
    learning_rate = trial.suggest_float("learning_rate", 0.01, 0.3)
    depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 8, 12)
    l2_leaf_reg = trial.suggest_float("l2_leaf_reg", 6, 25)

    params = {
        "iterations": 100,
        "learning_rate": learning_rate,
        "depth": depth,
        "l2_leaf_reg": l2_leaf_reg,
        "objective": "Cox",
        "bootstrap_type": "Bernoulli",
        "boosting_type": "Plain",
        "early_stopping_rounds": 50
    
    }
    
   
    event_occurred = Y > 0  # Positive Y values are events
    time_bins = pd.qcut(np.abs(Y), q=4, labels=False, duplicates='drop')  # Quantile-based binning

    # Combine event occurrence and time bins for stratification
    stratification_labels = np.array([f"{e}_{t}" for e, t in zip(event_occurred, time_bins)])

    # Initialize StratifiedKFold
    skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=2, shuffle=True, random_state=1997)
    
    fold_evaluations = []

    for train_index, test_index in skf.split(X, stratification_labels):
    
        X_train, X_test = X.iloc[train_index], X.iloc[test_index]
        Y_train, Y_test = Y[train_index], Y[test_index]
        
        # Update train_pool and test_pool for the current fold
        train_pool = Pool(X_train, label=Y_train, cat_features=cat_features)
        test_pool = Pool(X_test, label=Y_test, cat_features=cat_features)
        
        model = cb.CatBoostRegressor(**params)
        model.fit(train_pool, eval_set=test_pool, verbose=False)
        
        Y_pred = model.predict(test_pool)
        
        # Event indicator: True if the value in Y is positive
        event_occurred_test = Y_test > 0

        # Time duration: absolute value of Y
        time_duration_test = np.abs(Y_test)

        # Print the event indicator and time duration for debugging
        print("Event Indicator:", event_occurred_test)
        print("Time Durations:", time_duration_test)

        
        ci_test = concordance_index(time_duration_test, Y_pred, event_occurred_test)
        fold_evaluations.append(ci_test)

    average_ci_metric = np.mean(fold_evaluations)
    print(f"Average Concordance Index: {average_ci_metric}")
    return average_ci_metric


if __name__ == "__main__":
    study = optuna.create_study(direction="maximize")
    study.optimize(objective, n_trials=500)

    print("Number of finished trials: {}".format(len(study.trials)))
    print("Best trial:")
    best_trial = study.best_trial

    print("  Value: {}".format(best_trial.value))

    print("  Params: ")
    for key, value in best_trial.params.items():
        print("    {}: {}".format(key, value))


```

### Final CatBoost Survival Model

For survival analysis with CatBoost the best hyperparameters are actually no hyperparameters. Best concordance index was achieved with no additional Optuna hyperparameter tuning besides adjusting the number of iterations.


```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true

from catboost import CatBoostRegressor, Pool, FeaturesData

survival_model = CatBoostRegressor(
    loss_function='Cox',
    eval_metric='Cox',
    iterations=250,
    metric_period=25,
    verbose=True
)

survival_model.fit(train_pool, eval_set=test_pool)


```

### Model Accuracy (C-Index)

The C-statistic measures how well we can order people by their survival time (1.0 is a perfect ordering).

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| layout-ncol: 1
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true

# make the prediction using the resulting model
preds = survival_model.predict(test_pool)

```

```{r c-index}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true

library(survival)

predictions <- py$preds
event_times <- py$eval_test$days_total
event_observed <- py$eval_test$outcome_final

c_index <- survConcordance(Surv(event_times, event_observed) ~ predictions)
print(paste("Concordance Index (C-index):", c_index))

```

### Feature Importance

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| layout-ncol: 1
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true

from catboost import CatBoostRegressor, Pool, FeaturesData

gain = survival_model.get_feature_importance(prettified=True)

```

```{r feature-importance}
#| echo: true
#| layout-ncol: 1
#| label: tbl-feature-importance
#| tbl-cap: "Feature Importance"
#| tbl-subcap: 
#|   - "Gain"
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true

gain_tbl <- py$gain

table1 <- tibble( 'Feature ID' = gain_tbl$`Feature Id`,
                  'Importance' = gain_tbl$Importances) %>% 
  rowid_to_column(var = "Rank") %>% 
  as_hux() %>%
  theme_article() %>% 
  set_align(col=c('Rank','Importance'), value= "center") %>% 
  set_tb_padding(2)


table1

```


### Shapley Value Feature Importance Plots

CatBoost creates 4 different numerical encodings for each categorical feature by default. When we call get_feature_importances method we get aggregated across all the encodings the importance for the categorical feature.

The reason for grey color is either NAs or categorical values that cannot be scaled to high or low. <https://mlconference.ai/blog/tutorial-explainable-machine-learning-with-python-and-shap/>

<br>


```{python shap-setup}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: hide

import shap
import pickle

explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(survival_model)
shap_values = explainer(X)


# Save SHAP Explanation object
with open("data/shap_values.pkl", "wb") as f:
    pickle.dump(shap_values, f)
    
```

#### Beeswarm (Top 25 Features)

```{python shap-setup2}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: hide


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import shap
import pickle

plt.close()

with open("data/shap_values.pkl", "rb") as f:
    loaded_shap_values = pickle.load(f)

shap.initjs()

shap.plots.beeswarm(loaded_shap_values)
plt.tight_layout()

plt.savefig('images/shapley_beeswarm_chart.png', dpi=1200)

```


![Beeswarm Chart](images/shapley_beeswarm_chart.png){#fig-shap-beeswarm-chart}


#### Bar Chart for Feature Importance

```{python shapley-bar-chart}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: hide
#| label: fig-net-effect
#| fig-cap: 
#|   - "Net Effect"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import shap
import pickle

shap.initjs()

plt.close()

with open("data/shap_values.pkl", "rb") as f:
    loaded_shap_values = pickle.load(f)

shap.plots.bar(loaded_shap_values, max_display=25)

plt.tight_layout()

# Save the image in high resolution
plt.savefig('images/shapley_bar_chart.png', dpi=1200)


```


![Feature Importance Bar Chart](images/shapley_bar_chart.png){#fig-shap-mean-bar-chart}


```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: hide

# Numeric Column Indexes
numeric_column_indexes <- which(sapply(features_cb , is.numeric))

# Convert to Python index format (zero-based)
python_numeric_column_indexes <- numeric_column_indexes - 1

# Factor Column Names
factor_column_names <- names(features_cb)[sapply(features_cb, is.factor)]

# Numeric Column Names
numeric_column_names <- names(features_cb)[sapply(features_cb, is.numeric)]


```


#### Beeswarm by Numerical Features Only

```{python beeswarm-numerical-features}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: hide
#| out-width: 70%
#| out-height: 70%
#| label: fig-numerical-effects
#| fig-cap: 
#|   - "Numerical Effects"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import shap
import pickle

shap.initjs()

plt.close()

with open("data/shap_values.pkl", "rb") as f:
    loaded_shap_values = pickle.load(f)

num_features = r.numeric_column_names

# Subset the SHAP values to only include the numerical features
numerical_shap_values = loaded_shap_values[:, num_features]

# Plot the beeswarm for only the numerical features
shap.plots.beeswarm(numerical_shap_values)

plt.tight_layout()

# Save the figure with desired DPI
plt.savefig('images/shapley_beeswarm_numerical.png', dpi=1200, bbox_inches="tight")

```



![Numerical Beeswarm Chart](images/shapley_beeswarm_numerical.png){#fig-shap-numerical-beeswarm-chart}

#### Force Plot for Key Features

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: hide
#| out-width: 70%
#| out-height: 70%
#| label: fig-force-plot
#| fig-cap: 
#|   - "Individual Effects"

# Visualize the SHAP values for a specific prediction
shap.initjs()

shap_values2 = explainer.shap_values(X)

shap.force_plot(explainer.expected_value, shap_values2[0,:], X.iloc[0,:], text_rotation=45, matplotlib=True)

plt.tight_layout()

# Save the figure with desired DPI
plt.savefig('images/force_plot.png', dpi=1200, bbox_inches="tight")

```
![Force Plot](images/force_plot.png){#fig-shap-numerical-beeswarm-chart}
